the plant cells and animal cells:
I. - Plant cells have well-developed centrioles,
while animal cells do not.
II. 	 Animal cells possess a cellulose cell wall,
whereas plant cells do not.
III. 	 Animal cells have well-developed  golgi
bodies but plant cells do not.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
A cell may be defined as a **unit of protoplasm** bound by a **plasma or cell membrane** and possessing a nucleus. **Protoplasm** is the life giving substance and includes the cytoplasm and the nucleus. The cytoplasm has in it organelles such as ribosomes, mitochondria, golgi bodies, plastids, lysosomes and endoplasmic reticulum. Plant cells have in their cytoplasm large vacuoles containing non-living inclusions like crystals and pigments. The bacteria have neither defined cell organelles nor a well formed nucleus.
● **Plant cells do not have centrioles.** The centriole is absent in most plant cells, although it may be present in some lower plant forms in a rudimentary form. **In contrast, animal cells possess well-** **developed centrioles**, which play a crucial role in cell division by forming the spindle apparatus. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● **Plant cells have a rigid, cellulose-based cell wall** **external to the plasma membrane,** which provides structural support and shape. **Animal cells, on the other hand, lack a cell wall;** their outermost covering is the flexible plasma membrane. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Golgi bodies are present in both plant and animal cells.** In plant cells, the Golgi apparatus is organized into **smaller units called dictyosomes,** while in animal cells, it appears as a more compact and well-developed structure with **stacked cisternae.** Though structurally different, both perform similar functions such as protein modification, packaging and secretion. **So, statement III is not** **correct.**
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
Mitochondria and chloroplasts are considered
semi-autonomous organelles.
**Statement II:**
Mitochondria and chloroplasts are incapable of
independent existence outside the cytoplasm.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is **not**
correct
options.d:
Statement I is **not** correct but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Mitochondria (found in plant and animal cells) are the energy releasers and the chloroplasts (found only in green plant cells) are the energy trappers.
● **Mitochondria and chloroplasts** are termed **semi-autonomous organelles** because they possess some characteristics of independent organisms. Both organelles contain their own genetic material in the **form of DNA and have their own ribosomes,** enabling them to synthesize certain proteins independently. This allows them to self-replicate within the cell without requiring direct instructions from the cell nucleus for all their functions. Their ability to produce energy, mitochondria releasing energy through cellular respiration and chloroplasts trapping solar energy for photosynthesis also highlights their functional autonomy within the cell. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Despite having their own DNA and ribosomes, mitochondria and chloroplasts cannot** **survive independently outside the cell cytoplasm for an extended period.** This limitation is because the majority of the proteins and enzymes they require for their full function are encoded by nuclear DNA and synthesized in the cytoplasm. They rely on the cell’s nuclear machinery for these critical components, making them dependent on the host cell for survival and functioning over time.
● **The term “semi-autonomous” arises precisely because mitochondria and chloroplasts have** **their own DNA and protein synthesis machinery** but still depend on the cell for many proteins necessary for their maintenance and function. This partial autonomy allows them to carry out some functions independently, such as replication and limited protein synthesis, but they cannot completely sustain themselves outside the cellular environment. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
stem cell classification:
I. - Pluripotent stem cells can become any cell
type in the embryo and placenta.
II. 	 Totipotent stem cells can form all embryonic
and extra-embryonic tissues.
III. 	 Multipotent stem cells can differentiate cell
types within a particular lineage or family.
IV. 	 Unipotent stem cells can form one cell type
but cannot self-renew.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Stem cells** are unique, unspecialised cells with the remarkable ability to develop into various specialised cell types in the body. Unlike mature cells that have fixed roles, stem cells can both self-renew—dividing to produce more stem cells—and differentiate into a range of cells such as **muscle, nerve, blood, or** **skin cells.** They play a critical role in embryonic development, where a single fertilised zygote gives rise to the entire organism through successive cell divisions and differentiation.
● On the basis of the ability of self-replication and differentiation into various tissues, stem cells have been categorised into totipotent, pluripotent, multipotent and unipotent.
● **Pluripotent stem cells have the potential to differentiate into nearly all cell types of the** **embryo,** arising from all three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm and endoderm. **However, they** **cannot form extra-embryonic tissues such as the placenta.** These cells are derived from the inner cell mass of the blastocyst stage of the embryo. Unlike totipotent cells, pluripotent stem cells cannot give rise to a complete organism because they cannot form the placenta or other supporting tissues outside the embryo. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Totipotent stem cells** are the most versatile type of stem cells. They have the unique ability to **differentiate into all cell types** that form the entire organism, including both **embryonic tissues** **and extra-embryonic structures such as the placenta** and **yolk sac.** For example, the zygote and all cells of the early embryo, up to the eight-cell stage, are totipotent, meaning they can give rise to a complete, functional organism**. So, statement II is correct.**
● **Multipotent stem cells** can differentiate **into multiple, but limited, cell types within a** **particular lineage or family.** For instance, hematopoietic stem cells are multipotent and can develop into various types of blood cells, including red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets. Similarly, neural stem cells can generate several types of nerve cells. Multipotent stem cells are more specialised than totipotent or pluripotent stem cells and are typically found in adult tissues where they help maintain and repair specific tissues**. So, statement III is correct.**
● **Unipotent stem cells** can only differentiate into **one specific cell type, their own,** but unlike differentiated cells, **they retain the ability to self-renew.** This self-renewal capability allows them to maintain their population and support tissue regeneration. Examples of unipotent stem cells include epidermal stem cells and muscle stem cells. Despite their limited differentiation potential, unipotent stem cells play a crucial role in tissue maintenance and repair. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Prebiotics are dietary fibres that are degraded by gut microbiota.
II. 	 Probiotics are live microbes found in fermented foods like yogurt.
III. 	Prebiotics are non-digestible and serve as food for probiotics.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Prebiotics** are specific types of **dietary fibers** that resist digestion in the stomach and small intestine and reach the large intestine intact. There, they are **fermented or broken down by gut microbiota** (beneficial bacteria), which use them as food to grow and produce health-promoting metabolites. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **Probiotics are live microorganisms,** including specific strains of bacteria and yeasts, that when consumed in adequate amounts confer health benefits to the host. These beneficial microbes naturally occur in fermented foods like **yogurt, sauerkraut, kimchi, kefir and kombucha. So, statement** **II is correct.**
● Prebiotics are non-digestible components of food, primarily certain types of dietary fibers, that **selectively stimulate the growth** and activity of beneficial bacteria in the gut. **By serving as a** **nutrient source for probiotics,** prebiotics help sustain a diverse and healthy gut microbiome. The fermentation of prebiotic fibers by probiotics produces short-chain fatty acids like butyrate, which nourish the cells lining the colon and help maintain gut barrier integrity. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Gland Function
I. - Pineal Gland It secretes a melatonin hormone.
II. - Thyroid Gland It regulates the basal metabolic rate.
III. - Pancreas It acts as both an exocrine and an endocrine gland.
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pineal Gland:** It is located on the **dorsal side of the forebrain**. Pineal **secretes a hormone called** **melatonin.** Melatonin plays a very important role in the regulation of a 24-hour (diurnal) rhythm of our body. For example, it helps in maintaining the normal rhythms of sleep-wake cycle, body temperature. In addition, melatonin also influences metabolism, pigmentation, the menstrual cycle as well as our defense capability. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Thyroid Gland:** It is composed of **two lobes** which are located on either side of the **trachea**. Both the lobes are interconnected with a thin flap of connective tissue called **isthmus.** The thyroid gland is composed of **follicles and stromal tissues.** Iodine is essential for the normal rate of hormone synthesis in the thyroid. Deficiency of iodine in our diet results in **hypothyroidism** and enlargement of the thyroid gland, commonly called **goitre.** Thyroid hormones play an **important role in the regulation of the** **basal metabolic rate**. These hormones also support the process of red blood cell formation. Thyroid hormones control the metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins and fats. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● **Pancreas:** It is a composite gland which **acts as both exocrine and endocrine gland.** The endocrine pancreas consists of **‘Islets of Langerhans’.** There are about 1 to 2 million Islets of Langerhans in a normal human pancreas representing only 1 to 2 per cent of the pancreatic tissue. The two main types of cells in the Islet of Langerhans are called **α****-cells and** **β****-cells.** The α-cells secrete a hormone called **glucagon,** while the β-cells secrete **insulin. So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, all three of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With reference to microbodies, consider the following statements:
I.  	 They are small, single-membrane-bound cell organelles, present only in eukaryotic cells.
II. 	 They contain enzymes that are involved in metabolic activities such as detoxification.
III. 	 Peroxisomes and glyoxysomes are examples of microbodies.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Microbodies are small, single-membrane-bound cell organelles present only in eukaryotic** **cells.** They are usually present near the Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER). **So, statement I is correct.**
● Based on their functional properties, microbodies are classified into two types: peroxisomes and glyoxysomes. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Peroxisomes** are small, membrane-bound organelles. They are involved in energy metabolism in the cell, thus serving as a site for enzymes involved in metabolic reactions. They are derived from ER and replicate by fission. Unlike mitochondria and chloroplasts, peroxisome lacks their own genome. In animal cells, peroxisomes perform two major functions: **oxidation and lipid biosynthesis**. They contain oxidases that help in peroxide production, and catalase that neutralises the harmful products of the oxidation reaction. In liver cells, peroxisomes also help in alcohol **detoxification**. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
○ In plant cells, peroxisomes have two major roles to play, i.e., the conversion of fatty acids into carbohydrates in seeds and photorespiration in leaves.
● **Glyoxysomes** are specialised peroxisomes found in fungi and other higher plants (especially in fat storage tissues in germinating seeds). When oil-filled seeds germinate, the number and activity of glyoxysomes also increase. Glyoxysomes contain all the enzymes necessary for the fatty acid oxidation, glyoxylate cycle and gluconeogenesis. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are examples of
carnivorous plants?
I. - Water lily
II. 	 Sundews
III. 	 Cobra lily
IV. 	 Corkscrew
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Carnivorous plants** obtain some nutrients by **trapping and digesting various invertebrates** and occasionally even small frogs and mammals. Because insects are one of the most common prey items for most carnivorous plants, they are sometimes called **insectivorous plants.** Carnivorous plants have features to attract, trap, kill and digest prey and absorb nutrients. A number of plants have only some of these characteristics.
● Most plants absorb nitrogen from the soil through their roots. But carnivorous plants absorb nitrogen from their animal prey through their leaves, specially modified as traps.
● Among carnivorous plants, there are five different ways in which they capture their prey. These trapping mechanisms are **pitfall traps, flypaper traps, snap traps, bladder traps and lobster pot traps.**
○ **Venus flytraps** are examples of snap traps. A bit like a mousetrap, the hinged leaves will snap shut when a prey animal touches one of the sensitive hair-like projections on the inner surface of the leaf. The prey is then trapped behind the flytrap’s spiny ‘teeth’ and is subsequently digested by red sap. ○ **Bladderworts on the other hand**, as the name might suggest, are examples of bladder traps. These traps work by creating an internal vacuum in their ‘bladders’ by pumping out water and then literally sucking in prey through hinged trapdoors. In the aquatic species, the doors have long trigger hairs which work to release the vacuum mechanism when triggered by small invertebrates.
● **Sundews are flypaper traps**, using sweet and sticky secretions to attract and ensnare their invertebrate prey. The sticky substance is produced by glands on their tentacle-like leaves, which move in response to contact with prey, coiling inwards to further trap and smother it. Rainbow plants are another flypaper trap. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Pitfall traps of pitcher plants** are leaves folded into deep, slippery pools filled with digestive enzymes. **Cobra lily** is also a pitfall trap. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Lobster-pot traps of corkscrew plants** are twisted tubular channels lined with hairs and glands. **So,** **point IV is correct.**
● **Water lilies (****Nymphaea****) are not carnivorous plants.** They are flowering plants that live in freshwater environments and are known for their beautiful floating leaves and flowers. **So, point I is** **not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Information and Communication Technology, Robotics, AI
questions.questionText:
An employee at a corporate office opens an
email attachment from an unknown sender.
Within minutes, access to important files is lost
and a message appears on the screen demanding
payment in cryptocurrency to unlock the files. The
message warns that failure to pay within 48 hours
will result in permanent deletion of data.
Which of the following best explains the type of cyberattack involved in this situation?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ransomware** is a type of **malicious software** that **blocks access to a computer system** or **encrypts its files,** demanding a ransom for restoration. It typically displays **on-screen alerts claiming** **the system is locked or data is encrypted.** Victims are told that access will be denied unless they pay a specified amount, often between $200 and $400, usually in virtual currency like **Bitcoin.** Ransomware attacks have been observed for years and are a common form of cyber extortion.
● Ransomware is often spread through p**hishing emails that contain malicious attachments or** **through drive-by downloads.** Drive-by downloading occurs when a user unknowingly visits an infected website and then malware is downloaded and installed without the user’s knowledge.
● **Crypto ransomware,** a malware variant that encrypts files, is spread through similar methods and has also been spread through social media, such as Web-based instant messaging applications. Additionally, newer methods of ransomware infection have been observed. For example, vulnerable Web servers have been exploited as an entry point to gain access to an organisation’s network. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● A **Denial of Service (DoS) attack** is a cyberattack aimed at making a computer or network resource unavailable to its intended users by **disrupting its normal** **functioning.** This is typically achieved by overwhelming the target with traffic or exploiting vulnerabilities that cause it to crash.
● **Adware** is software that **displays unwanted (and sometimes irritating) pop-up** **adverts** which can appear on your computer or mobile device. Adware normally comes in software/programmes that you download from the internet, usually freeware or shareware and it secretly installs itself onto your device without your knowledge.
● **Spyware** is a type of malicious software (malware) that collects information from a computing **system without consent.** Spyware can capture keystrokes, screenshots, authentication credentials, personal email addresses, web form data, internet usage habits and other personal information. The data is often delivered to online attackers who sell it to others or use it themselves for marketing or spam or to execute financial crimes or identity theft.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
In the context of plant cell structure, the term
‘tonoplast’ refers to:

options.a:
A selectively permeable membrane
surrounding the central vacuole
options.b:
A membrane covering the cell wall in plant
cells
options.c: The inner membrane of the mitochondria

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Vacuoles:** They are **membrane-bound** **intracellular organelle** found in the cytoplasm of most **plants, fungi and also some animal cells.** The term ‘vacuole’, meaning ‘empty’, comes from its transparent morphology and lack of cytoplasmic material.
● Its major function is storage, structural support and recycling. The number and size of vacuoles vary per plant cell. Young cells have a large number of small vacuoles. As the plant cell matures, the vacuoles amalgamate to form a large central vacuole that occupies almost 90 per cent of the cytoplasmic volume.
● The central vacuole contains **water, cell sap, solid inclusions** and other metabolites. Other types of vacuoles found in a plant cell are **lytic vacuoles, protein storage vacuoles (PSV) and other** **storage vacuoles**. The vacuoles are covered by a membrane called the **tonoplast.** The plasma membrane and the vacuolar membrane (tonoplast) act as **semipermeable membranes** and water is absorbed by osmosis. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Microalgae:
I. - They are photosynthetic organisms found in
both freshwater and marine ecosystems.
II. 	 Some microalgae lack lignin and cellulose,
making them suitable for biotechnological
applications.
III. 	 They help to purify water as they consume
nutrients and pollutants during their growth.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Microalgae** are a group of **autotrophic microorganisms** that live in marine, freshwater and soil ecosystems and produce organic substances in the process of photosynthesis. Due to their high metabolic flexibility, adaptation to various cultivation conditions as well as the possibility of rapid growth, the number of studies on their use as a source of biologically valuable products is growing rapidly.
● Microalgae are microscopic photosynthetic organisms predominantly found in both **freshwater and** **marine ecosystems**. They are characterized by their **high chlorophyll content**, which allows for efficient photosynthesis. Unlike higher plants, some microalgae **lack lignin and cellulose**, making them more suitable for various biotechnological applications. **So, statements I and II are correct.**
● One significant advantage of microalgae is their ability to **treat industrial wastewater**. As they consume nutrients and pollutants during their growth, they help purify the water. At the same time, their biomass can be harvested for producing **bioenergy, bio-based chemicals** and other value-added products. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - All protozoans are heterotrophs and live as
predators or parasites.
II. 	 Lichens are the symbiotic association of
fungi and algae.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Protozoans** are **unicellular eukaryotic organisms** that do not possess chlorophyll or any other pigments necessary for photosynthesis. As a result, **they are heterotrophic**, meaning they depend on external sources of organic carbon for nutrition. They obtain food either by **engulfing other** **microorganisms (predators)** or by **invading host organisms and deriving nutrients from** **them (parasites)**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Fungi exhibit several types of ecological relationships. Most fungi are **saprophytes**, meaning they obtain nutrition from dead and decaying organic matter. Some fungi act as **parasites** on living plants and animals. However, **fungi can also form symbiotic associations**, most notably with **algae**, forming **lichens**— a mutualistic relationship where the fungal partner provides shelter, moisture and the algal partner performs photosynthesis. Thus, **some fungi** live as symbionts in association with algae, forming lichens. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Phycomycetes** represent a group of lower fungi that includes genera such as Mucor, Rhizopus and Albugo. These fungi are typically found in **aquatic environments**, as well as on **decaying organic** **matter** like dead wood in **moist and damp conditions**, making them important decomposers. Some members, like Albugo, are **obligate parasites** on plants, meaning they can only survive by drawing nutrients from a living host. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Which of the following animals does not belong
to the same biological class as the other three?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Flying fox**, **Elephant** and **Monkey** all belong to the class **Mammalia**. Mammals are characterised by the presence of **mammary glands** that produce milk to nourish their young. These three are **placental mammals**, meaning they give birth to live young that have developed inside the mother's uterus and are nourished through a placenta. They have **hair on their skin**, **external ears (pinnae)** and a **four-chambered heart**. Mammals are **warm-blooded (homoiothermous)** and typically give birth to live young (**viviparous**), with some exceptions. They can live in diverse habitats and have limbs adapted for various modes of movement, including flying (as in bats), swimming, or walking.
● The **wombat** is a **marsupial** and while it also belongs to the class **Mammalia**, it is **not a placental** **mammal**. Wombats belong to the order **Diprotodontia**, native to Australia. Marsupials give birth to underdeveloped young that continue to grow inside a **pouch on the mother’s body**. **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText:
With the present state of development, drones can effectively perform which of the following tasks?
I. - Aerial spraying of pesticides in agricultural fields
II. 	 Delivering medical supplies to remote or disaster-affected areas
III. 	 Conducting real-time surveillance and reconnaissance in border regions
IV. 	 Mapping inaccessible terrains for infrastructure development
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **A drone** refers to an aerial vehicle that receives remote commands from a pilot or relies on software for autonomous flight. Many drones display features like cameras for collecting visual data and propellers for stabilising their flight patterns. Sectors like videography, search and rescue, agriculture and transportation have adopted drone technology.
● **Drones have proven to be beneficial to agriculture,** presenting farmers with several ways to optimize their farms to maximise efficiency and reduce physical strain. Carrying out field surveys, seeding over fields, **spraying pesticides,** tracking livestock and estimating crop yield are all made easier through the use of UAVs while saving agriculture professionals valuable time. **So, point I is correct.**
● Drones can bypass difficult terrain and congested roads, enabling rapid delivery of **medical supplies**, **vaccines and emergency aid** to isolated communities and disaster zones. **So, point II is correct.**
● Drones equipped with cameras and sensors can provide **real-time surveillance and reconnaissance** **in border areas,** enhancing border security and monitoring. Drones can offer a comprehensive view of border regions, aiding in the detection of illegal activities, smuggling, and potential security threats. **So, point III is correct.**
● Drones can **capture high-resolution imagery and data,** enabling **accurate mapping of** **inaccessible terrains** **for infrastructure development project**s. This detailed mapping data facilitates better planning and design of roads, bridges, power lines, and other infrastructure projects. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText: Which one of the following correctly represents the increasing order of pH of the given substances?

options.a: Stomach acid < Fresh milk < Pure water < Lime water
options.b: Fresh milk < Stomach acid < Lime water < Pure water
options.c: Pure water < Stomach acid < Lime water < Fresh milk
options.d: Lime water < Fresh milk < Stomach acid < Pure water

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **pH scale** is a measure used to determine the **concentration of** **hydrogen ions (H**⁺**)** in a solution. The term 'pH' comes from the German word ‘potenz’, meaning power. This scale typically ranges from **0 to 14,** where:
● A **pH of 7** indicates a **neutral solution** (like pure water),
● A **pH less than 7** signifies an **acidic solution** and
● A **pH greater than 7** indicates a **basic (alkaline) solution.** The pH value reflects the nature of a solution:
● The lower the pH, the higher the concentration of hydronium ions (H₃O⁺), indicating stronger acidity.
● Conversely, as the pH increases beyond 7, the hydroxide ion (OH⁻) concentration rises, signifying greater alkalinity.

**So, the correct increasing order of pH of the given substances is Stomach acid < Fresh milk < Pure** **water < Lime water.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Small Modular Reactors (SMRs):
I. - They are advanced nuclear reactors with a power capacity of one-third of the generating capacity of traditional nuclear power reactors.
II. 	 SMR power plants have longer refueling intervals as compared to conventional reactors.
III. 	 They utilize nuclear fusion in a controlled manner to generate heat to produce energy.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Small Modular Reactors (SMRs)** are advanced nuclear reactors that have a power capacity of up to 300 MW(e) per unit, which is **about one-third of the generating capacity of traditional nuclear** **power reactors. So, statement I is correct.**
● SMRs, which can produce a large amount of low-carbon electricity, are: ○ **Small:** physically a fraction of the size of a conventional nuclear power reactor. ○ **Modular:** making it possible for systems and components to be factory-assembled and transported as a unit to a location for installation. ○ **Reactors:** harnessing **nuclear fission** to generate heat to produce energy. **So, statement III** **is not correct.**
● Given their smaller footprint, SMRs can be sited at locations not suitable for larger nuclear power plants. Prefabricated units of SMRs can be manufactured and then shipped and installed on site, making them more affordable to build than large power reactors, which are often custom-designed for a particular location, sometimes leading to construction delays.
● In areas lacking sufficient lines of transmission and grid capacity, SMRs can be installed into an existing grid or remotely off-grid, as a function of their smaller electrical output, providing low-carbon power for industry and the population.
● SMRs have reduced fuel requirements. Power plants based on SMRs may require **less frequent** **refuelling,** every 3 to 7 years, in comparison to between 1 and 2 years for conventional plants. Some **SMRs are designed to operate for up to 30 years without refuelling. So, statement II is** **correct.**
● **Bharat Small Modular Reactors:** India is actively exploring SMRs as a crucial part of its energy transition strategy, aiming to achieve net-zero emissions while ensuring energy security.
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText: Which of the following is an example of a physical change?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Properties such as **shape, size, colour and state of a substance** are called its **physical properties.** A change in which a substance undergoes a change in its physical properties is called a **physical change**. A physical change is generally reversible. In such a change, **no new substance is formed.**
● A change in which **one or more new substances are formed** is called a **chemical change.** A chemical change is also called a chemical reaction. All new substances are formed as a result of chemical changes. For example, digestion of food in our body, ripening of fruits, fermentation of grapes, etc., happen due to a series of chemical changes.
● **Melting of ice** is a **physical change** because it involves a change in the **state of matter**, from solid (ice) to liquid (water), without altering the **chemical composition** of the substance. The process is **reversible** and no new substance is formed. However, **rusting of iron**, **burning of paper** and **cooking of food** are all **chemical changes**, as they involve the formation of new substances with different chemical properties and are generally irreversible. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Global Positioning System (GPS), consider the following statements:
I. - It requires signals from a minimum of four satellites to accurately determine the precise location of a receiver on Earth.
II. 	 It can be used for accurate time stamping of stock market transactions.
III. 	 Its signals can be disrupted or degraded by atmospheric conditions such as ionospheric disturbances and solar flares.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a constellation of satellites that orbit approximately **11,000** **miles above the Earth** and transmit radio wave signals to receivers across the planet. By determining the time that it takes for a GPS satellite signal to reach the receiver, one can calculate its distance to the satellite and figure out the exact location on the Earth.
● GPS satellites have very precise clocks that tell time to **within 40 nanoseconds or 40 billionths** **(0.000000040) of a second.** There are also clocks in the GPS receivers. Radio wave signals from the satellites travel at 186,000 miles per second. To find the distance from a satellite to a receiver, use the following equation: (186,000 mi/sec) x (signal travel time in seconds) = Distance of the satellite to the receiver in miles.
● In addition to **longitude, latitude and altitude**, the GPS provides a critical fourth dimension – time. Each GPS satellite contains multiple atomic clocks that contribute very precise time data to the GPS signals. It takes **four GPS satellites to calculate a precise location on the Earth using the GPS**: **three to determine a position on the Earth** and one to **adjust for the error in the receiver's** **clock. So, statement I is correct.**
● Precise time is crucial to a variety of economic activities around the world. Communication systems, electrical power grids, and financial networks all rely on precision timing for synchronisation and operational efficiency.
● GPS provides a **highly accurate and synchronised time source**, which is crucial for timestamping trades and ensuring the integrity of financial transactions. **This precision is essential in high-** **frequency trading and other financial applications** where even minor timing discrepancies can have significant financial implications. **So, statement II is correct.**
● GPS signals can be significantly **disrupted by atmospheric conditions, particularly by ionospheric** **disturbances and solar flares.** These disturbances can cause errors in GPS positioning and even lead to signal loss. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: Which of the following animals are warmblooded?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Cold-blooded animals**, also known as **thermoconformers,** are **unable to regulate their internal** **body temperature independently.** Instead, their body temperature varies with that of their environment. To manage their temperature, they rely on external sources of heat—**basking in the sun** **raises their body temperature,** while seeking shade or burrowing lowers it.
● In response to temperature fluctuations, many **cold-blooded species adjust their metabolic rate.** For instance, **snakes, lizards and insects** often slow down their metabolism significantly in colder months, entering states like **torpor or diapause,** which resemble hibernation.
● Some of the most common cold-blooded animals include fish, **crocodiles, sharks,** **tortoises,** reptiles, insects, **frogs, Lizards and toads.** Cold-blooded animals are incredibly diverse and live in many different habitats, though they are not as widespread as warm-blooded animals.
○ When temperatures drop, **cold-blooded animals become sluggish and less active** due to their dependence on external heat sources. For example, **insects may lose the ability to fly** because their wing muscles cannot contract rapidly enough in the cold. Some species, like certain **moths**, counter this by **vibrating their wing muscles**—a process similar to human shivering—
which generates enough heat to enable flight. ○ Similarly, a **grasshopper** may be too cold and stiff to hop after a chilly night. But once it absorbs **warmth from the sun**, it regains mobility and can leap around normally.
● **Warm-blooded animals**, or **thermoregulators,** can **maintain a stable internal body** **temperature regardless of external conditions.** They possess physiological and behavioural mechanisms for temperature control. For example, they shiver to generate heat when cold and sweat or pant to dissipate heat when it's hot.
○ Like all birds, **penguins are warm-blooded.** Penguins have unique ways of keeping their bodies warm. Living in Antarctica, where temperatures can reach extreme lows of around -40 degrees Celsius (-40 degrees Fahrenheit), they need to maintain a consistent body temperature of around
37.8 to 38.9 degrees Celsius (100 to 102 degrees Fahrenheit) to survive. Penguins also need to **produce body heat to incubate their eggs.** After mating, a female will lay an egg and then pass it to the male to keep warm. ○ **Whales** have a similar default body temperature to humans—around 37 degrees Celsius (98.6 degrees Fahrenheit). To prevent loss of heat in the cold ocean waters, whales have a **thick layer** **of insulating blubber.** These fatty tissues and proteins also act as an energy reserve, allowing whales to go for extended periods without eating during migrations.
■ Whales also use **countercurrent heat exchanges** through a complex system of
arteries and veins to maintain their body temperature and prevent significant heat loss from areas that don’t have blubber, such as the fins. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText:
Which of the following makes use of the principle of multiple reflection of sound?
I. - Megaphones
II. 	 Stethoscopes
III. 	 Trumpets and shehnais
IV. 	 Design of ceilings in cinema halls
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:**
● **Sound bounces off** a solid or a liquid like a rubber ball bounces off a wall. Like light, sound gets reflected at the surface of a solid or liquid and follows the same laws of reflection. The directions in which the **sound is incident and is reflected make equal angles with the normal to the reflecting** **surface at the point of incidence** and the three are in the same plane. An obstacle of large size which may be polished or rough is needed for the reflection of sound waves.
● The principle of multiple reflection of sound refers to the **repeated bouncing of sound waves off** **hard surfaces,** which helps to concentrate or direct sound energy. The following makes use of the principle of multiple reflection of sound:
○ **Megaphones and loudhailers:** These devices use a conical shape to direct the sound waves through successive reflections along the inner surface, thereby preventing dispersion and concentrating the sound in a specific direction. This ensures the sound reaches a distant listener more clearly and loudly. **So, point I is correct.** ○ **Stethoscopes:** In a stethoscope, the sound of the heartbeat travels through a narrow tube and undergoes multiple reflections within it before reaching the doctor’s ears. This focused reflection helps in amplifying the internal sounds of the body. **So, point II is correct.** ○ **Musical instruments like trumpets and shehnais:** These instruments are designed with curved and narrow pipes that reflect the sound waves multiple times within the instrument before being emitted. This intensifies and enriches the sound, contributing to the unique tonal quality of the instrument. **So, point III is correct.** ○ **Design of ceilings in concert and cinema halls:** Generally, the ceilings of concert halls, conference halls and cinema halls are curved so that sound after reflection reaches all corners of the hall. Sometimes, a curved soundboard may be placed behind the stage so that the sound, after reflecting from the soundboard, spreads evenly across the width of the hall. **So, point IV is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: SDN-1, SDN-2 and SDN-3 technologies are mentioned in the context of:

options.d: Improving the efficiency of solar photovoltaic cells

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Genome editing** is a modern technique that allows scientists to make **precise changes to the DNA** **of living organisms.** About a decade ago, researchers in Germany and the US discovered a method to **‘cut’ DNA strands using Site-Directed Nucleases (SDNs)**, enzymes that act like molecular scissors. This breakthrough enabled scientists to alter specific parts of the genome to introduce desirable traits, especially in plants.
● One of the most advanced genome editing tools is the **CRISPR-Cas system (Clustered Regularly** **Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats–associated proteins),** which allows highly targeted modifications in DNA sequences. This technology has revolutionised plant breeding by enabling precise, efficient, and cost-effective edits.
● Genome editing techniques are categorised into three types: ○ **SDN1:** It introduces small insertions or deletions in the DNA without using any foreign genetic material. ○ **SDN2:** It involves minor edits using a short DNA template to guide the change, again without introducing alien genes. ○ **SDN3:** It involves the insertion of larger or foreign DNA segments, making it similar to genetically modified organism (GMO) technology. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs)** involve the modification of the genetic material of the host by the introduction of **foreign genetic material.** In the case of agriculture, soil bacteria are the best mining source for such genes which are then inserted into the host genome using genetic engineering. For example, in the case of cotton, the introduction of **genes cry1Ac and cry2Ab** mined from the soil bacterium **Bacillus Thuringiensis (BT)** allows the native cotton plant to generate endotoxins to fight pink bollworm naturally. BT Cotton uses this advantage to help farmers naturally fight the pink bollworm which is the most common pest for cotton farmers.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are the properties of X– rays?
I. - They affect the photographic plate.
II. 	 They cause fluorescence in chemical compounds.
III. 	 They ionise the gases.
IV. 	 They show no reflection in mirrors.
V. 	 They get deviated by an electric or magnetic field.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.c: I, II, III and IV only
options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● **X–rays** are produced when fast moving electrons are suddenly stopped by a heavy metal in a glass tube having **extremely low pressure**. The electrons emitted by the hot filament are focused on the target, which is made up of metal with a high melting point and a high atomic number. It covers wavelengths from about **10****–8** **m (10 nm) down** **to 10****–13** **m (10****–4** **nm).**
● **X-rays can blacken photographic film,** similar to visible light, but with greater penetrating power. This property is widely used in X-ray imaging and radiography, where the different absorption levels in body tissues create contrast on the film. **So, point I is correct.**
● X-rays can cause substances like **barium platinocyanide and zinc sulfide** to fluoresce (emit visible light). This property was instrumental in the discovery of X-rays by Wilhelm Röntgen and is still used in fluoroscopic imaging. **So, point II is correct.**
● X-rays are **ionising radiation**, meaning they can knock electrons off atoms and molecules, thereby ionising gases. This is a key reason why X-rays can be hazardous to living tissue and is also used in gas ionisation detectors. **So, point III is correct.**
● X-rays **show no reflection in mirrors,** no refraction in glass, no diffraction with the conventional gratings but when refined techniques are used with atomic layers of crystals, they show all these familiar phenomena of light. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **They don't get deviated by an electric or magnetic field.** X-rays are a form of electromagnetic radiation, which means they are neutral (carry no charge). Only charged particles get deflected by electric or magnetic fields. Hence, X-rays do not get deviated by such fields. **So, point V is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText: Three balloons, X, Y and Z, are each filled with a different gas: hydrogen, ammonia and carbon dioxide, respectively. If all three are released into the air at the same time, which one will ascend the slowest?

options.d: All will rise at the same rate

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● The rate at which a balloon rises in air depends on the **density or molar mass of the gas** it contains; **gases that are lighter than air tend to rise faster**. **Hydrogen**, used in **Balloon X**, has the **lowest** **molar mass** of approximately **2 g/mol,** making it the lightest and therefore the fastest to rise. **Ammonia**, in **Balloon Y**, has a molar mass of about **17 g/mol**, which is also lighter than air (average molar mass ≈ 29 g/mol), so it will rise, but more slowly than hydrogen. **Carbon dioxide,** used in **Balloon Z,** has a molar mass of around **44 g/mol,** making it heavier than air. As a result, **Balloon Z** **will rise the slowest among the three**, or may not rise at all, due to the greater density of carbon dioxide. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText: The electromagnetic waves suitable for the radar systems used in aircraft navigation are:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● **Microwaves (short-wavelength radio waves),** with frequencies in the gigahertz (GHz) range, are produced by special vacuum tubes (called klystrons, magnetrons and Gunn diodes). Due to their short wavelengths, **they are suitable for the radar systems used in aircraft navigation.** Radar also provides the basis for the speed guns used to time fast balls, tennis serves and automobiles.
● Microwave ovens are an interesting domestic application of these waves. In such ovens, the frequency of the microwaves is selected to match the resonant frequency of water molecules so that energy from the waves is transferred efficiently to the kinetic energy of the molecules. This raises the temperature of any food containing water. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Infrared waves** are produced by hot bodies and molecules. This band lies adjacent to the **low-frequency or long-wavelength** end of the visible spectrum. Infrared waves are sometimes referred to as **heat waves.** This is because water molecules present in most materials readily absorb infrared waves.
● **Radio waves** are produced by the accelerated motion of charges in conducting wires. They are used in radio and television communication systems. They are generally in the frequency range from 500 kHz to about 1000 MHz.
● **Ultraviolet rays** cover wavelengths ranging from about 4 × 10–7 m (400 nm) down to 6 × 10–10m (0.6 nm). Ultraviolet (UV) radiation is produced by **special lamps and** **very hot bodies.** The sun is an important source of ultraviolet light.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText:
The phenomenon wherein the Sun appears to
rise earlier and set later than its actual geometric
position is primarily due to:

options.a: Dispersion of sunlight in the atmosphere
options.b: Scattering of light by atmospheric particles
options.d:
Refraction of sunlight in the Earth’s
atmosphere

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **d**
**Explanation:**
● **Atmospheric refraction** refers to the **bending of light** **as it passes through layers of the Earth's atmosphere** **with varying densities**. A common example is the flickering or wavering appearance of objects seen through hot air rising above a fire or radiator. In such cases, the air just above the heat source is hotter and less dense than the cooler air above it, resulting in a lower refractive index. Since the temperature and density of the air keep changing rapidly, the refractive index also changes continuously, causing the light rays to bend unpredictably. This leads to a shifting or flickering image of the object, demonstrating atmospheric refraction on a small scale.
● The **Sun is visible to us about 2 minutes before the** **actual sunrise** and **about 2 minutes after the actual** **sunset** because of **atmospheric refraction.** By actual sunrise, we mean the actual crossing of the horizon by the Sun. The time difference between actual sunset and the apparent sunset is about 2 minutes. The apparent flattening of the Sun’s disc at sunrise and sunset is also due to the same phenomenon. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - In transverse waves, particle displacement is
perpendicular to the wave direction, while in
longitudinal waves, it is parallel to the wave
direction.
II. 	 Transverse waves can travel only through
solids while longitudinal waves can travel
through solids, liquids and gases.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A wave is a disturbance or oscillation that transfers energy from one point to another without the net transport of matter. In a wave motion, particles of the medium oscillate about their mean positions while the disturbance (energy) moves through the medium.
● **Propagation of Transverse Waves:** It shows a mechanical model for wave propagation. It comprises a row of spherical balls of equal mass, evenly spaced and connected by identical springs.
● **Propagation of Longitudinal Waves:** In longitudinal waves, the displacement of particles is along the direction of wave propagation. The hollow circles represent the mean positions of equidistant particles in a medium. The arrows show their (rather magnified) longitudinal displacements at a given time. Difference between the Transverse Waves and Longitudinal Waves:
**Transverse waves** **Longitudinal waves**
● Displacements of the particles are **perpendicular to the direction** **of propagation of the wave.**
● Displacements of the **particles are along the** **direction of propagation of the wave,** where the particles of the medium v**ibrate back and** **forth parallel to the direction** the wave is traveling. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Transverse waves look as crests and troughs propagating in the medium
● Longitudinal waves give the appearance of alternate compressions and rarefaction moving forward.
● Transverse waves **can only be** **transmitted in solids** or on the surface of liquids.
● Longitudinal waves can travel in **solids, liquids** **and gases. So, statement II is correct.**
● In case of a transverse wave, the displacement-distance graph gives the actual picture of the wave itself.
● In the case of longitudinal waves, the graph only represents the displacement of the particles at different points at a given time.
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to
the velocity of sound:
I. - Sound waves move faster through fresh water
than through seawater.
II. 	 Sound waves can be transmitted much more
efficiently through water than through air.
III. 	 The velocity of sound in water increases with
an increase in pressure and salinity.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● Sound waves are a type of mechanical wave that result from the vibration of particles in a medium. They require a material medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to propagate and cannot travel through a vacuum. Sound waves are classified as longitudinal waves.
● **Sound waves move faster through seawater than through freshwater**. This is because seawater has higher salinity and thus a higher bulk modulus (a measure of incompressibility), despite its slightly higher density. The increase in incompressibility outweighs the increase in density, leading to a greater speed of sound in seawater. On average, the speed of sound in **freshwater at 25°C is around 1497** **m/s,** whereas in **seawater at the same temperature it is around 1531 m/s.** **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● The **transmission of sound is much more efficient in water than in air** because of the difference in density and compressibility. Water is denser and less compressible than air, which makes it a better medium for sound wave propagation. The **speed of sound in air** (at 20°C) is **about 343 m/s,** whereas in **water it is about 1500 m/s** — more than four times faster. Also, due to higher acoustic impedance, sound loses less energy in water over distance, allowing it to travel much farther than in air. This principle is used in underwater sonar and marine navigation systems. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The velocity of sound in water is influenced by several environmental parameters**. With increasing depth, pressure increases, which compresses the medium and increases sound speed. Warmer water molecules vibrate faster, enhancing the wave propagation and thus increasing sound speed. Greater salinity increases the density and elasticity of water, leading to a higher speed of sound. **So, statement** **III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText: A child on a swing gains maximum height when pushed at regular intervals matching the swing’s natural frequency. This illustrates the principle of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:**
● The **phenomenon of resonance** is common among systems that tend to oscillate at a particular frequency. This frequency is called the **system’s natural frequency.** If such a system is driven by an energy source at a frequency that is near the natural frequency, the amplitude of oscillation is found to be large.
● This phenomenon illustrates the principle of resonance, which occurs **when an object is made to** **vibrate at its natural frequency by an external periodic force.** In the case of the child on a swing, the swing behaves like a simple pendulum with a natural frequency determined by its length and gravitational acceleration. When the child is pushed at regular intervals that match this natural frequency, the energy supplied by each push adds constructively, leading to **a gradual increase in amplitude** (i.e., the swing goes higher with each push). This is a classic example of **mechanical resonance**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Refraction:** It is the bending of a wave when it passes from one medium to another due to a change in its speed.
● **Interference:** it is the phenomenon where two or more waves superimpose to form a resultant wave of greater, lesser, or the same amplitude. It can be constructive (amplitudes reinforce) or destructive (amplitudes cancel).
● **Diffraction:** It refers to the bending and spreading of waves when they encounter an obstacle or pass through a narrow opening. It is more pronounced when the size of the obstacle or slit is comparable to the wavelength of the wave.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following is the objective of the BharatGen Programme?

options.a: To promote blockchain technology in financial transactions across India
options.b: To make generative AI accessible to citizens in various Indian languages
options.c: To develop quantum computing hardware for defence applications
options.d: To improve the effectiveness of satellite based navigation systems for urban transportation

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● **BharatGen** is a pioneering initiative designed to **democratize access to generative AI across** **India,** with a strong emphasis on producing **high-quality content in a wide range of Indian** **languages.** It is led by **IIT Bombay** under the **National Mission on Interdisciplinary Cyber-** **Physical Systems (NM-ICPS),** the programme aims to develop cutting-edge generative AI models capable of generating both textual and multimodal content.
● By addressing the country's rich linguistic diversity, BharatGen seeks to empower citizens through inclusive and language-aware AI solutions.
● **T-Hubs** play a central role in this initiative. A total of **four T-Hubs** have been established at key institutes across India, including:
○ Indian Institute of Science (IISc), Bengaluru ○ Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Madras ○ Centre for Development of Telematics, New Delhi ○ Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Bombay ○ Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Delhi. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Multimodal Artificial Intelligence Large Language Models:**
● These models mimic human-like perception and reasoning by integrating different sensory modalities, thereby enhancing the scope of natural language understanding, human-computer interaction, and context-aware decision-making.
● **Examples:** OpenAI’s GPT-4o, Google’s Gemini, and India’s BharatGen
**Relevance:** Recently, Union Minister of State (Independent Charge) for Science & Technology launched ‘Bharat Gen’, India’s first-of-its-kind, indigenously developed, Artificial Intelligence (AI) based, government-funded, Multimodal Large Language Model (LLM) for Indian languages.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Antimatter:
I. - They have the same mass as matter particles but carry opposite electric charges.
II. 	 They can be created both naturally and artificially.
III. 	 Both matter and antimatter annihilate each other and release energy when they come into contact.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Antimatter** is similar to ordinary matter in every aspect **except** **electric charge.** For example, the antimatter counterpart of a negatively charged electron is the **positron,**
which has the **same mass but a** **positive charge**. Neutral particles can have distinct antiparticles; for example, the neutron has an antineutron. However, scientists are still investigating whether neutrinos, tiny, neutral and elusive particles, are also their own antimatter counterparts. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● Antimatter particles have been **artificially created by scientists** using **ultra-high-speed collisions** in massive particle accelerators, such as the **Large Hadron Collider** near Geneva, operated by CERN (the European Organisation for Nuclear Research). At CERN, several experiments have successfully produced antihydrogen, the antimatter equivalent of hydrogen, and even antihelium, the most complex antimatter element synthesised so far.
● **Antiparticles also occur naturally in the universe**. However, when **matter and antimatter** **come into contact,** they **annihilate each other, releasing energy**. This annihilation explains why in a universe dominated by matter, antimatter cannot persist for long. **So, statements II and III are** **correct.**
● Antimatter lies at the core of one of the greatest mysteries of the universe. In the earliest moments after the Big Bang, the universe was filled with pure energy. As it cooled and expanded, equal amounts of matter and antimatter particles are believed to have formed.
● However, scientific measurements reveal that matter and antimatter behave almost identically in every detectable way. Given this symmetry, all matter and antimatter should have annihilated each other, releasing energy and leaving behind a universe devoid of any substantial matter. Yet a matter-dominated universe exists and the reason for this imbalance remains one of physics's unsolved puzzles. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, an international collaboration of scientists based in Europe reported that they had, for the first time, observed that the matter and antimatter versions of a type of subatomic particle called a baryon decay at different rates.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - GPT-4
II. 	 Gemini
III. 	 AlphaFold
IV. 	 Claude AI
V. 	 Llama 2
How many of the above are examples of Large Language Models (LLMs)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Large Language Models (LLMs)** are a category of foundation models trained on immense amounts of data, making them capable of understanding and generating natural language and other types of content to perform a wide range of tasks.
● They are trained on enormous amounts of data to provide the foundational capabilities needed to drive multiple use cases and applications, as well as resolve a multitude of tasks.
● They are easily accessible to the public through interfaces like OpenAI’s Chat GPT-3 and **GPT-4,** which have garnered the support of Microsoft. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Gemini is Google’s Large Language Model (LLM)**. It’s a family of multimodal AI models designed to process multiple modalities or types of data, including audio, images, software code, text and video. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Claude AI (Claude)** is a generative **Artificial Intelligence (AI) chatbot** and a **family of Large** **Language Models (LLMs)** developed by the research firm **Anthropic.** Claude excels at Natural Language Processing (NLP) and is multimodal: it accepts text, audio and visual inputs and can answer questions, summarise documents and generate long-form text, diagrams, animations, program code and more. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Llama 2** is a family of **pre-trained and fine-tuned Large Language Models (LLMs)** released by Meta AI in 2023. Released free of charge for research and commercial use, Llama 2 AI models are capable of a variety of Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks, from text generation to programming code. **So,** **point V is correct.**
● **AlphaFold is not a product of the LLM paradigm,** nor is it a simple application of transformers. It is a diffusion-based architecture enriched with evolutionary insights and tailored optimisations for biomolecular systems. **So, point III is not correct.** **So, only four of the above are examples of Large Language Models (LLMs).** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

Consider the following:
I. - Aircraft brakes
II. 	 Surgery and X-ray equipment
III. 	 Loudspeakers for Hi-fi equipment
IV. 	 Sporting goods
V. 	 Automobile hoods
In how many of the above applications are carbon
fibres used?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Carbon fibers** are fibers about **5–10 micrometres in diameter** and composed mostly of carbon atoms. Carbon fibers have several advantages, including high stiffness, high tensile strength, low weight, high chemical resistance, high temperature tolerance and low thermal expansion. The raw material used to make carbon fiber is called the precursor. About 90% of the carbon fibers produced are made from polyacrylonitrile. The remaining 10% are made from rayon or petroleum pitch. **Characteristics and Applications of Carbon Fibers:**
● Physical strength, specific toughness, light weight
● Aerospace, road and marine transport, **sporting** **goods. So, point IV is correct.**
● High dimensional stability, low coefficient of thermal expansion, and low abrasion
● Missiles, **aircraft brakes,** aerospace antenna and support structure, large telescopes, optical benches, waveguides for stable high-frequency (GHz) precision measurement frames. **So, point I** **is correct.**
● Good vibration damping, strength, and toughness
● Audio equipment, **loudspeakers for Hi-fi** **equipment,** pickup arms, robot arms. **So, point** **III is correct.**
● Electrical conductivity
● **Automobile hoods**, novel tooling, casings and bases for electronic equipment, EMI and RF shielding, brushes. **So, point V is correct.**
● Biological inertness and X-ray permeability
● Medical applications in prostheses, **surgery and** **x-ray equipment,** implants, tendon/ligament repair. **So, point II is correct.**
● Fatigue resistance, selflubrication and high damping
● Textile machinery, genera engineering
● Chemical inertness, high corrosion resistance
● Chemical industry; nuclear field; valves, seals, and pump components in process plants
● Electromagnetic properties
● Large generator retaining rings, radiological equipment
**So, in the making of all of the above carbon fibers are used.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the International Space Station (ISS):
I. - It is a multinational collaborative project
involving space agencies of the USA, Russia,
Europe, Japan and Canada.
II. 	 It orbits the Earth at an altitude of
approximately 36,000 kilometers in
geostationary orbit.
III. 	 It is primarily used for scientific research in
microgravity and space-based technology
development.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **International Space Station (ISS)** is a large, habitable spacecraft that orbits the Earth, functioning both as a residential outpost for astronauts and cosmonauts and as a state-of-the-art space laboratory. It represents an international collaboration among five major space agencies: **NASA (USA),** **Roscosmos (Russia), ESA (Europe), JAXA (Japan) and CSA (Canada).** The ISS is composed of multiple interconnected modules, each designed for a specific function, such as research, habitation, docking, or power generation. **So, statement I is correct.**
● It is the largest human-made structure in space, first launched on November 20, 1998. It functions as an orbiting habitat and research facility for astronauts from around the world. Since 2011, the ISS has remained continuously inhabited, highlighting its importance as a long-term space laboratory.
● **International Space Station (ISS)** orbits the Earth at an **altitude of approximately 400** **kilometers,** placing it in **Low Earth Orbit (LEO).** It travels at an impressive speed of about 28,000 km/h, completing one full orbit around the Earth every 90 minutes. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The ISS aims to: ○ Advance scientific **understanding of space and microgravity,** ○ Support innovative **space-based experiments across disciplines. So, statement III is** **correct.**
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Indian astronaut Group Captain Shubhanshu Shukla was selected as part of the Axiom Mission 4 to the International Space Station (ISS).

questions.questionText:
The architecture in which a generator and
a discriminator neural network are trained
simultaneously in a zero-sum game framework is
known as:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a **deep learning architecture**. It trains **two neural** **networks to compete against each other** to generate more authentic new data from a given training dataset. For instance, you can generate new images from an existing image database or original music from a database of songs. A GAN is called **adversarial** because **it trains two different networks** **and pits them against each other.** One network generates new data by taking an input data sample and modifying it as much as possible.
● Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are based on **the concept of a two-player zero-sum** **game,** wherein a **generator** network attempts to **produce data** indistinguishable from real samples, while a **discriminator** network evaluates and distinguishes between **real and generated data.** Through this adversarial process, GANs aim to learn the underlying data distribution and generate new, realistic samples from it.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Deepfakes utilize machine learning
algorithms while shallowfake relies on basic
video editing tools to modify the content.
II. 	 Deepfakes and shallowfakes both require a
basis of existing media content.
III. 	 Detecting deepfakes requires software while
shallowfakes can be identified through
editing errors.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Media manipulation technologies such as **deepfakes** and **shallowfakes** challenge the authenticity of digital content and pose serious risks to information integrity.
● **Shallowfakes** refer to manipulated media content created without the use of advanced artificial intelligence or machine learning technologies. These alterations are typically carried out using **conventional video or audio** **editing software,** making them accessible to a broader segment of the population.
● Deepfakes represent a more technologically advanced form of media manipulation. Leveraging deep learning and **complex machine learning algorithms**, particularly **deep neural networks,** deepfakes can produce hyperrealistic content that is often i**ndistinguishable from** **authentic footage.** These tools can convincingly replicate facial expressions, voice patterns and physical gestures, enabling the creation of synthetic media that can be both persuasive and deceptive.
● **Shallowfakes** are typically created through **manual editing processes**, relying heavily on the skills of the editor. Deepfakes are the product of **automated AI algorithms** that can learn and adapt, creating increasingly realistic fakes over time. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Deepfakes and shallowfakes **both require a basis of existing media content.** This content is then altered or repurposed to create a new narrative, often misleading in nature. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● Detecting deepfakes often requires **sophisticated software capable of analysing subtle** **inconsistencies** in digital media. In contrast, **shallowfakes** can be identified through simpler means, such as **inconsistencies in lighting or editing errors**. However, as technology advances, distinguishing between real and fake content becomes increasingly challenging, necessitating more advanced detection methods. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
